_meta.description: Global academic literature on Qatar transportation/infrastructure (papers by non-Qatar-based authors or international collaborations). Filtered 2018-2026. Collected 2026-05-12.
_meta.limitations: Google Scholar not directly queryable; relying on surface web search of ScienceDirect/MDPI/ResearchGate/IEEE results. Paywalled abstracts mostly visible. Several results are from Qatar-based authors but published in international journals — those have been kept here when international visibility is part of the relevance signal.

papers.title: Spatiotemporal analysis of road network evolution and urban growth in rapidly developing cities: The case of Doha metropolitan
papers.abstract_snippet: Uses dot density maps, isochrone ORS analysis, GIS Geohash Density Mapping. Identifies 3 factors: megaprojects/connectivity, national policy/land use, population/road network coverage. Concludes ideal growth direction is west and north.
papers.methodology: GIS-based spatiotemporal analysis, isochrone analysis (walk/cycle/car), geohash density mapping
papers.data_source: OSM road network + planning documents + population data
papers.key_finding: Doha's road network growth has not been aligned with population centers; recommends infill west/north

papers.title: Driverless Metro Service in Doha, Qatar Expected to Reduce Daily Traffic by 190,000 Private Automobiles and CO2 Emissions by 19.42 Kilo Tons
papers.abstract_snippet: Projects metro displacement of ~190k cars/day, 19.42 kt CO2/yr, 3.15 t PM10, 11.45 t SO2 reductions.
papers.key_finding: Projected metro impact; not measured ex-post

papers.title: Social impact assessment towards sustainable urban mobility in Qatar: Understanding behavioral change triggers
papers.abstract_snippet: Behavioral change triggers for sustainable mobility shift in Qatar.
papers.key_finding: Identifies psychological and socioeconomic triggers for modal shift

papers.title: Evaluation of sustainable urban mobility using comparative environmental life cycle assessment: A case study of Qatar
papers.abstract_snippet: Compared LCA of automobiles vs metro: 78pp reduction in climate change impact, 100pp reduction in PM formation.
papers.key_finding: Metro has 78pp lower climate impact than autos

papers.title: Rapid urban development and national master planning in Arab Gulf countries — Qatar as a case study
papers.abstract_snippet: Master planning evolution in GCC with Qatar case. NPRP 5-074-5-015.

papers.title: Quantifying the impact of COVID-19 preventive measures on traffic in the State of Qatar
papers.abstract_snippet: Lockdown / restrictions effects on Qatar traffic flows.
papers.key_finding: Quantifies COVID traffic reduction in Qatar

papers.title: Sustainable Urban Mobility in Qatar: Transforming Doha's Built Environment through Transit-Oriented Development (TOD)
papers.abstract_snippet: TOD-based transformation of Doha's built environment.
papers.key_finding: Pushes TOD around metro stations as path to sustainable mobility

papers.title: Change analysis of land use/land cover and modelling urban growth in Greater Doha, Qatar
papers.abstract_snippet: Landsat-based LU/LC change detection for Greater Doha; urban growth modeling.
papers.methodology: Remote sensing + CA-Markov / urban growth modeling

papers.title: Rapid Urban Growth and Land Use Patterns in Doha, Qatar: Opportunities for Sustainability?
papers.institution: European Journal of Sustainable Development Research
papers.abstract_snippet: Land use patterns analysis of Doha for sustainability.
papers.key_finding: Documents land use sprawl patterns and sustainability tradeoffs

papers.title: The Impact of Rapid Motorization and Urban Growth: An Analysis of the City of Doha, Qatar
papers.abstract_snippet: INACCESSIBLE — title indicates motorization growth impact analysis.

papers.title: Factors Affecting Public Transportation Ridership in a High-income Developing Country
papers.key_finding: Key determinants of public transport ridership in Qatar identified

papers.title: Asphalt Rideability Specification and Construction of Expressways in Qatar (CIC 2020)
papers.abstract_snippet: International Roughness Index specification and construction quality for Ashghal expressway program.
papers.key_finding: IRI specification and Qatar expressway construction quality

papers.title: Who Is Going to Ride the Upcoming Metro in Qatar?
papers.abstract_snippet: Pre-launch projection of metro ridership demographics.
papers.key_finding: Demographic profiling of expected metro users

papers.title: Exploring the potential of traffic index data to analyze essential traffic impact in developing cities
papers.abstract_snippet: Discusses TomTom-style traffic index for developing cities (not Qatar-specific in surfaced content).
papers.key_finding: Frames traffic index data utility in developing cities

papers.title: Developing Assessment Framework for Strategic Transportation Projects in Qatar
papers.abstract_snippet: Transportation Assessment Framework developed for Qatar TMP appraisal — NOT ex-post evaluation.

globally_documented_principles_relevant_.principle: Fundamental Law of Road Congestion (Duranton & Turner, 2011): 1% increase in road capacity yields 1% increase in VMT — net congestion typically unchanged.
globally_documented_principles_relevant_.relevance: Qatar spent 95% of $100B transport investment on roads. No published ex-post analysis tests whether this law holds in Doha.

globally_documented_principles_relevant_.principle: GCC PIM efficiency gap (IMF 2023): GCC has 'mismatched funding needs and budget allocations; lack of ex-post evaluations of investments; inadequate performance data and reporting.'
globally_documented_principles_relevant_.relevance: Direct external endorsement of a gap. A Qatar-specific ex-post evaluation directly fills this acknowledged gap.

globally_documented_principles_relevant_.principle: Induced demand effect strongest in high-growth, low-density, car-dependent cities.
globally_documented_principles_relevant_.relevance: Doha matches this profile (sprawl, SUVs, weak transit competition); never tested empirically.

globally_documented_principles_relevant_.principle: Mode shift outcomes from new metro systems vary 10x across studies depending on context (HK 40%+, US Sun Belt <5%).
globally_documented_principles_relevant_.relevance: Doha Metro post-launch mode shift studied via surveys (RP/SP) but not via combined supply-demand panel.
